Tafseer:
The lesson from the mutual harmony of night and day:
'Duha' refers to the forenoon when the activities of day begin, and mankind, having rested during the night, enters the field of work refreshed.
The mutual harmony of night and day: The testimony of the night is presented in the Qur'an from various perspectives. Here, it is accompanied by: 'when it settles' (idha saja). 'Saja' means to settle and become still, which shows that the time of night meant here is when the noise of the day and the activities of the early part of the night have settled, and the night has become still, providing peace and rest. Thus, while 'Duha' refers to a specific part of the day, 'By the night when it settles' refers to the contrasting part of the night.
Though the night and day are different in their shape, appearance, temperament, and effects, man is dependent on both of them for his life, and this world is dependent on both for its survival. It is the great mercy of the Creator that He created the day alongside the night, and through their interaction, the purposes necessary for the survival of the world are fulfilled, as stated in Surah Yunus, verse 67:
(He is the One Who made for you the night that you may rest therein and the day giving sight.) (Yunus, 10:67),
and in Surah Al-Qasas, verse 73:
(And out of His mercy He made for you the night and the day that you may rest therein and [by day] seek of His bounty and that you may be grateful.) (Al-Qasas, 28:73).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 1 and 2 was combined in the original text.]

Both grief and comfort are for the training of man: This is the primary claim proved by the testimonies of nature. The meaning is: just as the light and heat of the sun, and the darkness and stillness of the night are necessary for physical life, similarly the trials of ease and difficulty, comfort and distress, and wealth and poverty are necessary for man's moral and spiritual training. Through these, Allah Almighty, as explained in Surah Al-Fajr, tests His servant's patience or gratitude. The meaning is: if you face opposition, scarcity of helpers, and lack of resources today, or if the divine help does not arrive as quickly as you desire, do not assume your Lord has abandoned you or is displeased; rather, this is a trial for your training so that you are fully prepared to fulfill your responsibilities.
This entire theme is implied within this verse, as is clear from the preceding and subsequent verses. However, for the sake of conciseness, the most prominent aspect of comfort was selected—namely, reassuring him that the circumstances he faces are not because of Allah's indifference or displeasure, but are for his training in accordance with the divine law.
It should noted that when the opposition of the Quraysh grew intense in the Meccan period, it caused you special anxiety—lest some error on your part was the cause of Allah's displeasure. This was described as a back-breaking burden in the subsequent twin Surah: (And We removed from you your burden which weighed heavily on your back) (Al-Inshirah, 94:2-3).
The actual cause behind your eager wait for revelation: In this state of anxiety, it was natural for you to eagerly await the divine revelation, as it was the only source of light in dark circumstances, and through it, you could assess whether you were performing your duty of communication in accordance with His pleasure. However, the revelation is subject to Allah's wisdom; it is not necessary that it descend whenever the Prophet is anxious. Thus, the delay in revelation would double his anxiety. This anxiety is mentioned in various mimes of the Meccan period.
The comfort given in this verse relates to these circumstances. It is not necessary that this verse was revealed in response to the polytheists' taunt: 'This person's Lord has abandoned him.' Did the polytheists believe that Allah sent him revelation? They called him a soothsayer and a poet. Furthermore, the experience of revelation was felt only by the Prophet, so how could the polytheists know whether it was descending or delayed? As far as communication is concerned, he never stopped it even for a single day, so there was no opportunity for them to say he had abandoned his mission.

Further explanation of the comfort: Here, 'akhirah' (the latter) and 'oola' (the first) do not refer to their theological meanings (the world and the Hereafter), but to the latter phase and the first phase of the mission—namely, its future and its present.
This explains the comfort, showing that the present circumstances will change, and the future will be far better than the past and present. The Qur'an has given this glad tiding in various places, and in the ancient scriptures, the beginning of the call was compared to a mustard seed which is small but grows to become a great tree where birds find rest.
A comprehensive glad tiding: The word 'khayr' (better) used here is a comprehensive glad tiding, encompassing the victory of Islam, the conquest of Mecca, the destruction of the enemies, and the multitudes entering the religion—as mentioned in the previous Surahs, pointed to in the next Surah, and detailed in Surah An-Nasr.

It is stated: very soon your Lord will give you and you will be pleased. Although what He will give is not specified, context indicates it refers to the 'better' promised in the preceding verse, which encompasses all the victories and successes Islam attained in the subsequent phases. Since these matters were still in the unseen, the second object of 'yu'teeka' (will give you) was omitted, but 'fa-tarda' (and you will be pleased) showed a glimpse of that glorious future—meaning He will give you so much that you will be pleased! This single word contains what cannot be contained in a book.

Pointing to some of the Prophet's life experiences: To confirm what was stated in verse 4—namely, your future will be better than your past—attention is drawn to the Prophet's own life, which serves as an excellent illustration of this reality.
First, He referenced his being an orphan. Orphanhood is itself a major difficulty, but in a corrupt society like the one described in the previous Surahs, there was no place of respect for orphans, as stated:
(No! But you do not honor the orphan) (Al-Fajr, 89:17),
and elsewhere, pointing to some members of his family:
(For that is the one who drives away the orphan) (Al-Ma'un, 107:2).
But Allah's special favor was upon the Prophet: although his father left no wealth, his grandfather and then his uncle took care of him with respect and affection. Under normal circumstances, a grandfather loving his orphan grandson and an uncle caring for his orphan nephew is a natural thing; but in a corrupt society, as noted, this is rare, and cannot occur unless Allah Almighty casts His love upon the orphan, as He did with Moses, who was raised by his greatest enemy—Pharaoh—in his palace.

'And He found you lost and guided [you]' (wa wajadaka dallan fa-hada) - This describes the spiritual favor he received in the subsequent phase.
A seeker's search and God's guidance: You know that your sound nature could never be satisfied with the ancestral practices and traditions of your family, yet there was no other source of light to satisfy you. The followers of past religions around you had corrupted their beliefs and deeds so completely—as clarified in the Madinese Surahs—that no seeker of truth could obtain guidance from them. This situation placed you in a severe mental conflict, described as 'lost' (dallan). 'Dall' here does not mean astray, but a seeker of the path. The prophets are born upon the sound nature (fitrah) and never deviate from its obvious truths even in their youth. However, nature can guide only regarding the general beliefs; it cannot explain the details of beliefs, their requirements, or prescribe the correct boundaries of morals and actions. Therefore, despite being upon the sound nature, a person depends on knowing: what are His attributes and His demands? what are His rights on the servant and how are they to be fulfilled? how to organize life so that it is entirely approved by the Creator? Unless these questions are answered, true peace of mind cannot be attained, nor can one's relationship with the Lord be established. These were the questions that dominated the Prophet's heart during the phase of his life to which 'And He found you lost' points. This was neither a state of deviation nor a state of guidance, but was the searching of a seeker—like a person standing at a crossroad, unable to decide which direction to choose. Before his mission, in the isolation of the cave of Hira, he remained absorbed in resolving these questions.
The state of the followers of the Abrahamic faith before the mission: Before the mission, some of the followers of the Abrahamic faith in Arabia were in such a state of confusion that they would sit in the Kaaba, saying with regret: 'O our Lord! We do not know how to worship You; otherwise, we would have done so.' The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) must have been in a similar state until he was introduced to the reality of faith and the Book, as stated in Surah Ash-Shura, verse 52:
(And thus We have revealed to you an inspiration [i.e., Qur'an] of Our command. You did not know what is the Book or [what is] faith, but We have made it a light by which We guide whom We will of Our servants.) (Ash-Shura, 42:52),
and in Surah Yusuf, He described this state as 'heedlessness':
(And indeed, you were, before it, among the heedless [unaware]) (Yusuf, 12:3).

The source of true wealth is faith and the gnosis of Allah: 'And He found you poor and made [you] self-sufficient' (wa wajadaka 'a'ilan fa-aghna) - It is stated: We found you poor and made you self-sufficient. True wealth (ghina) depends more on the state of the heart than on material resources. If a person's chest is devoid of faith, he is poor even if he possesses the treasures of Qarun; and if his chest is filled with faith, he is wealthy even if he wears a simple cloak like John, feeding on wild honey and locusts. This wisdom is stated as:
(True wealth is the wealth of the heart).
This true wealth is produced by faith, the gnosis of Allah, and the light of His Book, without which a person can never be free of greed, and the bowl of a greedy person is never filled:
'The bowl of a greedy person is never filled.'
This world is a world of causes; therefore, man is dependent on causes. If the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) benefited from the wealth of Khadijah, it was the great fortune of Khadijah and her wealth; but the wealth (ghina) mentioned here is not produced merely by wealth, but is the fruit of the guidance mentioned in the preceding verse, whose correct expression is 'the expansion of the breast' (sharh-i-sadr), which is explained in the subsequent twin Surah—Al-Inshirah. Those who have regarded this self-sufficiency as the result of Khadijah's wealth have looked only at the surface, whereas the actual reality is beyond what is seen. We content ourselves with these brief references; this will be discussed further.

Fulfilling the right of blessings: Warning to the powerful of the Quraysh: This describes the right of the blessings mentioned above, containing a subtle rebuke to those who, having received His favors, grew arrogant. It is stated: do not adopt their way, but since your Lord gave you shelter when you were an orphan, show compassion, mercy, and care to orphans and protect their rights. We explained under Surah Al-Fajr, verse 19, that in Jahiliyyah, the powerful relatives would usurp the rights of orphans and daughters. 'So do not oppress' (fa-la taqhar) points to this situation, meaning: do not exploit the weak state of the orphan to usurp his rights. This warning was not given because there was any risk of the Prophet committing such a crime, but is a warning to those powerful Quraysh who were criticized in the previous Surahs but opposed the Messenger instead of reforming. In this Surah, ignoring them, the Prophet is instructed: 'Let them do what they do; you must protect the rights of orphans.'

Fulfilling the right of the second blessing: 'And as for the petitioner, do not repel [him]' (wa amma-s-sa'ila fa-la tanhar) - This describes the right of the blessing mentioned in: 'And He found you lost and guided [you]'. 'Sa'il' (petitioner) is used in a wide sense here—referring to one who asks for his physical needs, one who asks regarding some mental or intellectual confusion, or one who asks regarding his religion. It is stated: help and guide them to the best of your ability, and if unable, make a polite excuse, without rebuking or commanding them. The meaning is: remember that a period of time passed over you when you were full of questions that caused you distress, until your Lord removed your confusion and answered your questions. Its right is that you treat petitioners with goodness, unlike those who, having received His favors, treat the poor with harshness, and if they face any adversity, claim: 'My Lord has humiliated me,' forgetting how they humiliated His servants.

Share Allah's wisdom with people: 'But as for the favor of your Lord, proclaim [it]' (wa amma bi-ni'mati rabbika fa-haddith) - This describes the right of the blessing mentioned in: 'And He found you poor and made [you] self-sufficient'. We explained that this refers primarily to the wisdom of religion and the Shariah, whose status is described as:
(and whoever has been given wisdom has certainly been given much good) (Al-Baqarah, 2:269).
Note the word 'fahhaddith' (proclaim/speak). It is suitable for the blessing of wisdom, not merely for wealth. It is stated: proclaim the treasury of wisdom your Lord has blessed you with—meaning just as your Lord blessed you with it freely, share it with others freely, proclaim it, and fill every assembly with its remembrance.
